rationale: Ahluwalia et al. (2001) is cited in the conceptual framework and in Study 3 development, not only in a generic literature list. The citing paper uses it for specific claims that commitment can limit negative spillover and that diagnosticity/involvement matter when processing negative information, then operationalizes related constructs (e.g., commitment, self-brand connection, diagnosticity) in its empirical tests. While the 2025 article does not directly reproduce the 2001 model, it clearly transfers that commitment-spillover logic to a new digital brand safety context.
relation_description: The citing paper applies Ahluwalia et al.'s commitment-based spillover insight to brand safety incidents, using it to justify testing whether commitment-related factors buffer negative adjacent-content effects. It also draws on the cited paper's diagnosticity-related reasoning when evaluating alternative mechanisms.
